title: Immanuel part 3
scripture_text: “The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel” (which means “God with us”).
devotional:
How can we know our Immanuel? How can we move from assuming that God is with

us to experiencing God with us?

Experiencing the presence of God begins with a choice. We must choose to believe in and recognise His presence. Have you ever entered a room and you have tried to ignore someone, or for some reason you have just not realised they were there. That is how we often we react or respond to the presence of God. We can either ignore him, or assume that he is not there.

Years ago when I lived and worked in Donegal a group of people from a different church came to see us. I went into the room where they were, and guess who was the first person I looked for? Caroline. Why? because I loved her and was excited that she was there. And that is something like how we should be thinking about Immanuel. Every room we walk into in life, the first thing we should do is look for Immanuel, the presence of the living God.

Often when life is very difficult we can pursue solutions, we want to fix things.

Or we look for answers or explanations, and we ask questions like, What is God

doing? Or, what is God saying? These are great questions to ask, however,

sometimes we need ‘presence’ before we need solutions or answers. Our hearts

need to engage with the one who loves us more than anyone else.

Experiencing the presence of God happens when we recognise that God wants to encounter us in all of life. When it comes to our understanding of ‘spirituality’ in our western thinking, we usually have two main categories, spiritual and unspiritual. We compartmentalise our lives into ‘God categories,’ and ‘worldly categories,’ This is known as Platonic thinking were you have areas of our lives that are set apart for God and the other parts God has no interest in. Often we are not conscious of thinking this way. This subliminal thinking will create in us a default position that says something like ‘I don’t expect to encounter God at….’Biblical spirituality is the antithesis to platonic thinking, for it believes that all of life is about God and in every area of life he can be encountered and known.

Are there areas of your life where you don’t really expect to experience Immanuel? For example, when you got to work or school, do you ever think that you might encounter God today? Or do you have no expectation or desire for such an encounter? You may be out for coffee, or working in the garden, or having family time at home, or out on a date with your partner, or a million other scenarios, but they all contain the potential of having an encounter with Immanuel.

Immanuel comes to us so we can become the people we are meant to be. If you were to really think about it, there is nothing demeaning or diminishing about enjoying the presence of God. In fact the complete opposite happens, the experienced presence of God encourages and animates a person. We are never more alive than when our lives are touched by his presence. It is in His presence where our true identities are discovered. It is in accepting His presence that we are free to be who we really are. God will manifest his presence in our lives through the work and ministry of the Holy Spirit, and it is through his presence God manifests the intensity of His love.

Sometimes we need to create a ’sacred space’ were we prioritise drawing near to God, so he can pour his love into our hearts. As we draw to God, he draws near to us, and it then we discover the glory of being who we were meant to be.

We must not understand Immanuel just as someone who helps us in our ‘crisis management,’ or who ‘enables us for ministry,’ He does do that, but primarily we need to understand Immanuel, God with us, as the core essential for life.

O God please be with us.

my life.
prayer: Father I acknowledge that I need to experience your presence in every area of
html:
Immanuel part 3

Study Text: Matthew 1:23
“The virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and they will call him Immanuel” (which means “God with us”).

How can we know our Immanuel? How can we move from assuming that God is with

us to experiencing God with us?

Experiencing the presence of God begins with a choice. We must choose to believe in and recognise His presence. Have you ever entered a room and you have tried to ignore someone, or for some reason you have just not realised they were there. That is how we often we react or respond to the presence of God. We can either ignore him, or assume that he is not there.

Years ago when I lived and worked in Donegal a group of people from a different church came to see us. I went into the room where they were, and guess who was the first person I looked for? Caroline. Why? because I loved her and was excited that she was there. And that is something like how we should be thinking about Immanuel. Every room we walk into in life, the first thing we should do is look for Immanuel, the presence of the living God.

Often when life is very difficult we can pursue solutions, we want to fix things.

Or we look for answers or explanations, and we ask questions like, What is God

doing? Or, what is God saying? These are great questions to ask, however,

sometimes we need ‘presence’ before we need solutions or answers. Our hearts

need to engage with the one who loves us more than anyone else.

Experiencing the presence of God happens when we recognise that God wants to encounter us in all of life. When it comes to our understanding of ‘spirituality’ in our western thinking, we usually have two main categories, spiritual and unspiritual. We compartmentalise our lives into ‘God categories,’ and ‘worldly categories,’ This is known as Platonic thinking were you have areas of our lives that are set apart for God and the other parts God has no interest in. Often we are not conscious of thinking this way. This subliminal thinking will create in us a default position that says something like ‘I don’t expect to encounter God at….’Biblical spirituality is the antithesis to platonic thinking, for it believes that all of life is about God and in every area of life he can be encountered and known.

Are there areas of your life where you don’t really expect to experience Immanuel? For example, when you got to work or school, do you ever think that you might encounter God today? Or do you have no expectation or desire for such an encounter? You may be out for coffee, or working in the garden, or having family time at home, or out on a date with your partner, or a million other scenarios, but they all contain the potential of having an encounter with Immanuel.

Immanuel comes to us so we can become the people we are meant to be. If you were to really think about it, there is nothing demeaning or diminishing about enjoying the presence of God. In fact the complete opposite happens, the experienced presence of God encourages and animates a person. We are never more alive than when our lives are touched by his presence. It is in His presence where our true identities are discovered. It is in accepting His presence that we are free to be who we really are. God will manifest his presence in our lives through the work and ministry of the Holy Spirit, and it is through his presence God manifests the intensity of His love.

Sometimes we need to create a ’sacred space’ were we prioritise drawing near to God, so he can pour his love into our hearts. As we draw to God, he draws near to us, and it then we discover the glory of being who we were meant to be.

We must not understand Immanuel just as someone who helps us in our ‘crisis management,’ or who ‘enables us for ministry,’ He does do that, but primarily we need to understand Immanuel, God with us, as the core essential for life.

O God please be with us.

my life.

Prayer: Father I acknowledge that I need to experience your presence in every area of
Bible in a year reading plan: Job 29 – 32
